title: Meeting Economics

content.question: Of every meeting you attended this week, which produced a decision or deliverable? Which didn't?
sop: Audit every meeting on your calendar this week and label each with the decision or deliverable it produced, or none.
Cancel or convert to async (a doc, a recording, a message) any meeting that consistently produces none.
For meetings that stay, require an agenda with a stated decision to be made, no agenda, no meeting.
Rate Output 1-10 and log Time in hours for your recurring meetings (see calculation, page 6)
Re-audit monthly, meetings tend to creep back without a regular cut.
content.explainer: Most meetings are the lazy alternative to a well-written doc. Justify each one with an output or cancel it.
questions: Of every meeting you attended this week, which produced a decision or deliverable? Which didn't?
Which recurring meeting on your calendar hasn't produced a real decision in the last month?
If you canceled your least useful weekly meeting, what would you actually lose?

definitions.def: Asynchronous — communication that doesn't happen live, like a doc, recording, or message people read whenever they can, instead of gathering for a meeting.

content.equation_example: Rate Output 1-10 (value of decisions or deliverables produced) and log Time in hours spent. Output 6, Time 2 hours → MMS = 6 ÷ 2 = 3. Higher MMS means the meeting produces a lot for the time spent; lower means it's a low-value time sink.
content.closing_statement: A meeting without a decision is a cost.

caption:
Principle Nº 19: Meeting Economics

Meetings are investments. Most meetings are the lazy alternative to a well-written doc.

Ask yourself: Of every meeting you attended this week, which produced a decision or deliverable? Which didn't?
